Footer.tagline: Decision systems for teams that ship.
Footer.signOff: Built by humans who write Rust on weekends.

Home.metaTitle: Hiram — Decision systems that turn data into revenue
Home.metaDescription: Real-time decision engines, AI query infrastructure, and event-driven systems shipped to production.

hero.headline: Systems that turn data into automated decisions.
hero.subhead: We build the layer between your events and your business outcomes. Real-time decision engines, AI query infrastructure, and event-driven architectures that replace dashboards and manual ops with software that decides.
hero.trust: SHIPPED IN PRODUCTION FOR TELCO, FINTECH, AND RETAIL OPERATORS HANDLING 10M+ EVENTS/DAY.

whatWeDo.headline: We are not an agency. We build decision systems.
whatWeDo.lede: Most teams don't need another dashboard. They need software that takes an event, applies the right logic, and triggers the right action — without a human in the loop. That's what we build, and that's all we build.

rows.title: Decision engines, not workflow builders.
rows.body: You bring the rules, the constraints, and the SLAs. We build the engine that runs them deterministically at scale, with full audit trails and shadow-mode rollouts.

rows.title: AI where it makes the call, not where it makes a demo.
rows.body: LLMs and ML models embedded inside the decision path — not bolted onto a chat window. Query layers over your operational data so analysts and systems get answers in seconds.

rows.title: Event infrastructure that survives Monday morning.
rows.body: Kafka, Redpanda, Kinesis, NATS — we design the topology, the schemas, the consumers, and the dead-letter strategy. So when volume spikes, nothing silently drops.

demo.headline: Describe the problem. Get the system.
demo.lede: Plain-English business problem in. Diagnosis, suggested system, an example decision rule with a real JSON payload, and an effort/impact bracket out. Same prompt discipline we ship to production.

cards.title: Decision Engines
cards.body: Deterministic rule engines, ML-scored decisions, hybrid pipelines. Versioned rules, shadow deploys, full replay against historical events.

cards.title: AI Query Layer
cards.body: Natural-language queries over your operational data, with structured outputs your systems can act on. Not a chatbot — an API.

cards.title: Event Infrastructure
cards.body: Topologies, schemas, consumers, DLQs. Kafka, Redpanda, Kinesis, NATS. We pick the boring tool when boring is correct.

caseStudiesPreview.headline: Systems running in production right now.
caseStudiesPreview.lede: Anonymized at the client's request. The architectures and metrics are real.

cards.title: Real-time fraud scoring for a LATAM telco
cards.summary: Replaced a legacy rule pack with a hybrid Rust engine. p99 latency 480ms → 80ms, false positives down 23%.

cards.title: Credit pre-approval engine for an EU fintech
cards.summary: Replaced a 14-step manual review with a hybrid ML+rules engine. Decision time fell from 36h to 90s.

cards.title: Dynamic pricing engine for a NA retailer
cards.summary: Hourly price decisions across 80k SKUs, driven by competitor signals and inventory pressure. +6.8% margin in 90 days.

finalCta.headline: If you have events, we can build the system that decides on them.
finalCta.lede: Tell us your event volume, your current decision latency, and what's blocking you. We'll tell you whether we're the right fit — or who is.

Capabilities.metaDescription: Decision engines, AI query layers, and event infrastructure. The three components Hiram builds — and how we build them.

hero.headline: Three things, done well.
hero.lede: Every decision system Hiram ships is some combination of the same three components: a decision engine that runs your business logic, an AI query layer that turns operational data into structured answers, and event infrastructure that doesn't lose messages. We don't build dashboards, we don't build chatbots, and we don't take on work that fits neither category.

decisionEngines.headline: The engine layer between your events and your business logic.
decisionEngines.lede: Every operational team eventually accumulates rules: approve this transaction, throttle that customer, route this case to a human. They live in spreadsheets, in Slack threads, in three-thousand-line if-statements buried in the API. A decision engine is the dedicated software that owns those rules — runs them deterministically, versions them like code, and gives you a replay trail when someone asks why a customer got the answer they got.

items.title: Rule compilation
items.body: Rules authored in a typed DSL or YAML, compiled to a deterministic decision graph. No interpretation at request time — the engine evaluates a compiled artifact.

items.title: Hybrid ML + rules pipelines
items.body: Models score features, rules gate the action. Score thresholds, override rules, and policy checks live alongside the model — not downstream of it.

items.title: Shadow + canary modes
items.body: Every new rule version runs in shadow against live traffic before it owns a single decision. Diff reports per cohort before promotion, canary by tenant or percentage when promoted.

items.title: Decision replay & audit
items.body: Every decision keeps the inputs, the rule version, the model version, and the path through the graph. Replay any decision against any past or future rule set.

decisionEngines.comparisonIntro: Most teams reach for a workflow tool, a BPM platform, or a hand-rolled service. Each falls short on at least one axis that matters when decisions are revenue-impacting.

aiQueryLayer.headline: Ask your operational data a question. Get a structured answer your system can act on.
aiQueryLayer.lede: Most AI-over-data products end at a chat window. We end at an API. Same LLM under the hood, but the contract is a typed object your downstream systems can consume — not a paragraph a human has to interpret. Schema-aware prompts, output validation, eval harnesses, and explicit fallback paths.

items.title: Schema-aware prompting
items.body: The model sees your data shape (table schemas, event shapes, business glossary) at request time. No hallucinated columns.

items.title: Structured output contracts
items.body: Every query type has a typed response schema (Zod / JSON Schema). Outputs are validated, retried on failure, surfaced as typed errors.

items.title: Eval harness per query type
items.body: We build a small golden dataset per query type and run it on every prompt change. CI fails on regressions you can see, before they ship.

items.title: Fallback paths and guardrails
items.body: Rate limits, timeouts, deterministic fallbacks. The system degrades gracefully when the model is slow or wrong.

aiQueryLayer.limitsTitle: WHAT WE DON'T LET LLMS DECIDE
limits: Final authorization on irreversible actions (charges, terminations, payouts).
Anything regulated without an explicit rule overlay (credit decisions, refusal of service, account closure).
Anything where the cost of a wrong answer exceeds the cost of asking a human.

eventInfrastructure.headline: Event-driven systems that don't silently drop messages on Monday morning.
eventInfrastructure.lede: The decision engine is only as good as the event stream feeding it. We design the topology, the schemas, the consumers, and the failure-mode behavior. We pick the boring tool when boring is correct.

tooling.title: Kafka
tooling.body: When you have a platform team that already operates it, or you need the broadest connector ecosystem and tools you'll find on Stack Overflow at 2am.

tooling.title: Redpanda
tooling.body: When you want the Kafka API without ZooKeeper / KRaft ops, lower latency, and a single binary. Good default for greenfield.

tooling.title: Kinesis
tooling.body: When you're already deep in AWS and the tradeoffs (ordered shards, throughput-per-shard math) match your access patterns.

tooling.title: NATS / JetStream
tooling.body: When you want light ops, request-reply patterns, and JetStream gives you the durability + ordering you need.

always.title: Schema registry discipline
always.body: Every event has a versioned schema, breaking changes go through a contract review. Consumers fail loudly on incompatible payloads, never silently.

always.title: Exactly-once where it matters
always.body: At-least-once everywhere else. Idempotency keys on the action layer make the difference visible to consumers.

always.title: Backpressure by design
always.body: Producers slow down when consumers can't keep up. No unbounded queues. No surprise bills.

always.title: DLQ + replay strategy from day one
always.body: Failed events land in a dead-letter topic with original payload + failure context. Replay tooling is part of the deliverable, not a future ticket.

About.metaDescription: Hiram builds decision systems. A small team of senior engineers with product-company DNA. One technical point of contact, full IP transfer on completion.

hero.headline: Hiram builds decision systems. That's the whole company.
hero.lede: Small team of senior engineers. Product-company DNA. No agency-style account managers. Single technical point of contact from kickoff to handoff.

rows.title: A small team of senior engineers.
rows.body: 6+ years engineering experience minimum, several from staff/principal roles at scale-ups and infra companies. We don't subcontract the work that ships.

rows.title: Product-company DNA.
rows.body: We've built products that ship to thousands of users. We bring that discipline to client engagements: shadow rollouts, replay, observability, drift monitoring.

rows.title: One point of contact, technical from day one.
rows.body: No project managers translating between you and the engine. The engineer building the system is the one in the call.

items.title: 4-week target to first decision in production.
items.body: We design for shadow-mode validation in week 2-3, canary in week 4. If the architecture doesn't make that achievable, we tell you before contract.

items.title: Shadow-mode by default.
items.body: New engines run alongside existing systems for 1-3 weeks before traffic flips. Decisions are logged but not enacted. We compare and tune.

items.title: Monthly architecture review with the client.
items.body: Once live, a recurring 60-minute review with your engineering leadership. We bring the metrics. Cancel any time.

items.title: Full IP transfer on completion.
items.body: Code, infra-as-code, runbooks, eval datasets — yours. We're not in the lock-in business.

Contact.metaDescription: Tell us your event volume, your decision latency, and what's blocking you. We respond within 2 business days and tell you whether we're the right fit — or who is.
Contact.headline: Start a conversation.
Contact.lede: Tell us your event volume, your current decision latency, and what's blocking you. We'll tell you whether we're the right fit — or who is.
steps: We read your form within 2 business days. Real human, not a CRM trigger.
If it's a fit, we propose a 45-minute architecture call — no slides.
If it's not, we tell you why and refer you to someone who fits.

form.name: Full name
form.problem: What problem are you trying to solve?

form.budgetMicrocopy: Asked because we are not the right fit under ~30k EUR. We'd rather tell you now than waste your week.

form.successBody: We read every form within 2 business days. If you don't hear back, your filter rang the bell — check email.
